narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite challenges, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admission system uses a model trained on data from thousands of students to predict the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, and for you, it estimated a 51% chance of failure—meaning it saw you as more likely to struggle than succeed, tipping the scales against admission. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 4, a Year 3 decile of 3, lower-middle family income, an LSAT score of 23, an undergraduate GPA of 2.60, and plans to attend part-time, which differ from many applicants who often have higher averages like 5.71 for Year 1 deciles, 5.53 for Year 3, 36.70 LSATs, 3.21 GPAs, and mostly full-time status (92.7% of students). The biggest factor pushing the model toward predicting bar failure was your LSAT score, as it stood well below the typical levels that signal strong exam readiness for students like those in the dataset. On a more positive note, your lower-middle family income background actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success more than average cases might. Similarly, your Year 1 decile nudged things positively compared to peers, and your undergraduate GPA provided some helpful lift toward passing. However, your Year 3 decile contributed somewhat toward the failure prediction by being lower than typical, and choosing part-time status added a small push in the same direction, unlike the full-time path most students take. Overall, while some parts of your story showed promise, the standout lower LSAT score was the dominant reason the model leaned toward rejection. Compared to typical students who pass, your academics and test scores are in the lower range, which the system flags as higher risk. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that key score, strengthening your GPA through additional coursework, and exploring full-time options if feasible—these steps could dramatically shift future predictions in your favor. You're resilient, and with targeted improvements, law school doors can reopen for you. Keep pushing; your background as a Black woman brings invaluable perspectives the field needs.
